hero.heading: Ethereum for Institutions
hero.tagline: Production-grade, open settlement network for tokenized assets, stablecoins, and financial infrastructure.

numbers.heading: Key Statistics
numbers.networkSecurityLabel: Total value securing the network ({ethAmount} ETH)

useCases.heading: Institutional Use Cases
useCases.subheading: Explore Ethereum's digital asset landscape, from tokens to technologies

rwa.label: Tokenization
rwa.description: Tokenize assets, like real estate, commodities, or bonds, while stablecoins are designed for steady value, often pegged to assets like USD or gold.

defi.label: Decentralized Finance
defi.description: Open financial systems built on Ethereum. DeFi lets anyone lend, borrow, trade, and earn yield on their assets globally, and creates utility for tokenized assets.

privacy.label: Privacy & Compliance
privacy.description: Deploy on Ethereum's public Mainnet for global transparency, or use enterprise-grade privacy solutions for confidentiality and control.

layer2.label: Custom Environments
layer2.description: Layer 2s are customized blockchains built on top of Ethereum. Deployments can be open, or built for requirements like speed, brand, privacy, cost, and more.

leader.intro: Ethereum is an open, globally distributed settlement network that supports programmable financial activity. Unlike proprietary or permissioned systems, Ethereum is a neutral base layer, with no single operator, that institutions can build compliance-aware applications on.

resilience.label: Operational Resilience
resilience.description: Ethereum has maintained 10 years of uninterrupted uptime, including liveness through 15+ successful network upgrades. A diverse infrastructure set and open client ecosystem reduce infrastructure and single-operator risks.

settlement.label: Global Settlement
settlement.description: Ethereum settles value continuously across jurisdictions, with transparent, guaranteed finality and publicly verifiable execution.

neutrality.label: Neutrality
neutrality.description: No single point of failure, no central coordinator, no pause button, no counterparty risk. Resilient to geopolitical, regulatory, and infrastructure-level risks.

decentralization.label: Decentralization
decentralization.description: {securityValue}+ in economic security distributed across geographies and client implementations makes Ethereum the most expensive smart contract platform to attack.

liquidity.label: Liquidity and Utility
liquidity.description: The leading platform for asset tokenization, with 67% of all tokenized assets deployed. Ethereum's DeFi ecosystem benefits from 10 years of network effects with its DeFi ecosystem being 8x larger than that of the next largest ecosystem.

programmability.label: Programmability
programmability.description: Smart contracts enable automated issuance, transfer controls, lifecycle management, and conditional settlement. L2 ecosystems and apps can be highly customized to the needs and requirements of financial institutions.

composability.label: Composable Ecosystem
composability.description: Ethereum supports an ecosystem of wallets, custodians, auditors, data providers, and scaling solutions used globally in production by institutions today.

kendrick.role: Global Head of Digital Assets Research
kendrick.quote1: I think tokenised real-world assets will grow from $34bn today to $300bn over the next 12 months. All of this growth will happen on Ethereum because TradFi trusts Ethereum.
kendrick.quote2: It is irrelevant that other chains are faster or cheaper. Ethereum has been around for over 10 years and has never gone down. For TradFi, trustworthiness trumps marginal speed and cost savings every day of the week.

zschach.quote1: Saying Ethereum is the wrong blockchain because it has high gas fees is like saying Amazon shouldn't use the internet because dial-up was slow in 1995.
zschach.quote2: Banks aren't building on 2015 Ethereum, they're using today's Ethereum stack with tomorrows upgrades.

mitchnick.quote1: There was no question that the blockchain that we would start our tokenization on was on Ethereum.

tenev.quote1: I believe tokenization is the greatest capital markets innovation since the central limit order book.
tenev.quote2: The Robinhood Chain is the first Ethereum Layer 2 optimized for real-world assets.

scaling.heading: Ethereum is Constantly Evolving
scaling.description: Ethereum's active R&D roadmap delivers continuous upgrades, each bringing performance, security, and usability improvements relevant to institutional adoption.

pectra.title: Pectra
pectra.card1: Pectra introduced key improvements to Ethereum's staking infrastructure, including raising the validator cap from 32 to 2,048 ETH and enabling more flexible staking operations. For enterprises and institutional stakers, this means simpler validator management, lower operational overhead, and a more efficient staking experience at scale.

fusaka.title: Fusaka
fusaka.card1: Fusaka focused on dramatically reducing transaction costs and increasing network throughput through data scaling improvements. For businesses building on Ethereum, this translates to cheaper, faster transactions, making enterprise-grade applications and high-volume use cases significantly more viable.

postQuantum.title: Post-Quantum
postQuantum.card1: Ethereum has been preparing for the quantum computing threat since 2018, longer than any other blockchain ecosystem. The EF has committed $20M+ to formal verification and cryptographic research, with AI breakthroughs accelerating timelines by 100x. A dedicated post-quantum team was formed in early 2026, with 10 client teams now building quantum-resistant implementations. The full transition is targeted for 2028, well ahead of the estimated mid-2030s quantum threat, with zero fund loss and zero downtime.

glamsterdam.title: Glamsterdam
glamsterdam.card1: Glamsterdam is an upcoming upgrade focused on further optimising the Ethereum execution layer and network performance. For enterprises, this signals Ethereum's continued commitment to improving the base layer infrastructure that underpins institutional applications, DeFi protocols, and tokenised asset platforms.

cta.heading: Get In Touch
cta.description: We will answer your questions, help identify potential paths forward, provide technical support, and connect you with relevant industry leaders.

library.heading: Library
library.subheading: Explore the latest insights, reports, and analysis, from across the institutional landscape.

metadata.title: Ethereum for Institutions | The Institutional Liquidity Layer
metadata.description: Discover why leading institutions build on Ethereum. Unmatched resilience, deep liquidity, and proven security for the onchain economy. Explore use cases.

hero.heading: Tokenization
hero.description1: Ethereum is the market leader for asset tokenization, home to over 60% of all tokenized assets and over 60% of global stablecoin supply.
hero.description2: Institutions tokenize assets such as public and private securities and credit, offer 24/7 settlement, deploy programmable cash, implement customizable payment rails, and more, on the liquid, open, and resilient ecosystem of Ethereum and its Layer 2s.

overview.description: Institutions tokenize assets such as public and private securities and credit, offer 24/7 settlement, deploy programmable cash, implement customizable payment rails, and more, on the liquid, open, and resilient ecosystem of Ethereum and its Layer 2s.

infrastructure.heading: Ethereum as Financial Market Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Ethereum can help institutions, fintechs and corporates to improve operations, reduce costs and increase revenue:
infrastructure.depthDesc: Ethereum hosts by far the deepest liquidity of any onchain environment
infrastructure.scaleDesc: Ethereum's Layer 2s add low-fee rails for retail and high-frequency flows
infrastructure.complianceDesc: Programmable settlement for compliance, disclosures, and auditability
infrastructure.yieldDesc: Access familiar instruments, like T-bills and short-duration products

comparison.heading: Comparison Matrix
comparison.ethereum_neutrality: No single operator, thousands of nodes
comparison.l1Alt_neutrality: Single client, controlled treasury, low number of validator operators

categoryBreakdown.heading: Ethereum Leads in Tokenization
categoryBreakdown.description: Breakdown of tokenized asset categories on Ethereum.

stablecoins.heading: Stablecoins on Ethereum
stablecoins.marketCap: Total stablecoin market cap on Ethereum L1:

rwas.heading: Tokenized Assets on Ethereum
rwas.totalSector: Total tokenized asset sector on Ethereum L1:

cards.ustbDesc: {brand} Short Duration US Government Securities Fund

why.heading: Why Ethereum for Financial Market Infrastructure
why.description: Credible settlement, global reach. Ethereum pairs a maximally-neutral settlement layer with Layer 2 execution environments that deliver scale, cost efficiency, and customizability, so institutions can move cash-like value and short-duration assets with auditability and programmatic controls.
why.desc1: Ethereum pairs a maximally-neutral settlement layer with Layer 2 execution environments that deliver scale, cost efficiency, and customizability.
why.desc2: Institutions can move cash-like value and short-duration assets with auditability and programmatic controls.

l1.heading: Ethereum L1
l1.finalityDesc: High-value settlement, state roots for rollups, and durable records institutions can audit and attest against
l1.securityDesc: Assets can be stored in an environment that is built to withstand major catastrophes and geopolitical tensions
l1.riskGatingDesc: Keep complex or experimental logic off L1; use it for final settlement, collateral custody, and proofs

l2.heading: Ethereum's L2s
l2.throughputDesc: Rollups process transactions offchain, inherit L1 security, and deliver low fees suitable for payments, market-making, and high-frequency flows
l2.configurableDesc: L2s can add compliance features, like allowlisting or KYC'd pools, while remaining non-custodial and settling to L1
l2.specializationDesc: Multiple L2s let institutions segregate workloads, such as retail payments vs. treasury ops, without fragmenting trust, because settlement reconciles on L1

l2Section.heading: Ethereum's L2s: The Execution & Scale Layer
l2Section.description: Layer 2 networks extend Ethereum's tokenization capabilities with specialized execution environments.
l2Section.throughputDesc: Rollups process transactions offchain, inherit Ethereum's security, and deliver low fees suitable for payments, market-making, and high-frequency flows
l2Section.configurableDesc: L2s can add compliance features, like allowlisting or KYC'd pools, customizable currency for transactions while remaining non-custodial and settling to L1
l2Section.specializationDesc: Multiple L2s let institutions segregate workloads, such as retail payments vs. treasury ops, without fragmenting trust, because settlement reconciles on L1

metadata.title: Tokenization | Stablecoins & Asset Tokenization on Ethereum
metadata.description: Ethereum is the market leader for asset tokenization, home to over 60% of all tokenized assets and over 60% of global stablecoin supply.

hero.heading: Compliant Privacy on Public Rails
hero.description1: On Ethereum, institutions keep counterparties, data, and business logic confidential while settling against the deepest onchain liquidity in the world.
hero.description2: No trust assumptions. No vendor lock-in. No counterparty risk at the infrastructure layer.

solutions.heading: Privacy Solutions on Ethereum
solutions.description: Privacy is not a sidechain or a silo. It is a set of open standards, composable on L1 or L2, designed to meet regulatory, risk, and audit requirements. The Ethereum ecosystem offers multiple approaches to institutional privacy, each addressing different use cases.

prividium.description: Prividium architecture with offchain data availability and onchain proofs. Sub-second ZK finality with built-in KYC/SSO. Selective disclosure enables compliance proofs without revealing underlying transaction data.

programmablePrivacy.description: Complete privacy across transactions, state, and compute through private smart contracts. Client-side proof generation keeps sensitive data on user devices. Composable with the broader EVM ecosystem.
programmablePrivacy.example: Aztec (Boerse Stuttgart Confidential Tokenized Security Transaction), EY Nightfall, Miden

compliancePools.description: Prove funds are clean without revealing full transaction history. Designed from day one for the regulatory problem. Enables institutions to demonstrate AML compliance through cryptographic attestation rather than full data disclosure.
compliancePools.example: Privacy Pools (co-authored by Vitalik Buterin; live on mainnet)

shieldedTx.description: ZK privacy pools processing billions in shielded volume across Ethereum and its L2s. Mature middleware enabling private DeFi interactions including transfers, swaps, and lending — production-tested at scale.

emerging.description: Fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) enabling computation on encrypted data, encrypted mempools preventing MEV extraction, dark pool architectures for institutional trading, and privacy-preserving rollups. See the IPTF Privacy Map for a comprehensive overview.
emerging.example: Fhenix, Zama (FHE); Shutter Network (encrypted mempool); Renegade (dark pools); Miden (privacy rollup)

compliance.heading: Compliance Without Exposure
compliance.selectiveDisclosureDesc: Prove specific facts like eligibility, policy compliance, or solvency without publishing full transaction details. When deeper review is required, view keys / audit keys can enable scoped access for authorized parties.
compliance.privacyCredentialsDesc: zkID and attestations support KYC-style eligibility checks without exposing personal information onchain. Verify identity properties without creating data liabilities.
compliance.composablePrimitivesDesc: These primitives plug into multiple architectures, from prividiums to private smart contracts, so institutions can meet regulatory requirements while protecting counterparties and market-sensitive data.

whyMatters.heading: Why This Matters Beyond Features
whyMatters.resilienceDesc: No complete outage since genesis in 2015. AWS, Cloudflare, and Facebook have all gone down. Private chains have experienced multi-month outages. Ethereum is the most battle-tested settlement layer in production.
whyMatters.censorshipDesc: No single entity can block transaction inclusion. For cross-border operations with jurisdictional conflicts, this is an operational requirement, not a philosophical preference.
whyMatters.noCounterpartyDesc: On a private chain, your settlement layer is controlled by a company. On Ethereum, it is controlled by mathematics. No board decision, acquisition, or strategy pivot can change the rules.
whyMatters.economicsDesc: Transparent utility pricing with no lock-in. SaaS licensing for private chains scales with vendor leverage. Over a decade, the cost differential compounds significantly in Ethereum's favour.
whyMatters.interoperabilityDesc: Privacy solutions on Ethereum compose with the full DeFi stack: ~$55B in TVL, 1,700+ protocols, and the deepest onchain liquidity globally. Private chains offer none of this. Your assets are only as useful as one vendor's ecosystem allows.

trustVsCrypto.heading: Trust-Based Privacy vs Cryptographic Privacy
trustVsCrypto.description: Permissioned blockchains promise privacy through operator commitments. Ethereum delivers privacy through mathematics. For institutions with fiduciary duties, the distinction is material.
trustVsCrypto.tableTrustGuarantee: "I promise I won't look at your data."
trustVsCrypto.tableTrustMechanism: Operators and governance committees commit to respect data boundaries
trustVsCrypto.tableCryptoMechanism: Zero-knowledge proofs allow verification without disclosure
trustVsCrypto.tableTrustIncentives: The promise breaks. Governance changes, regulatory demands, or operator pivots can override privacy guarantees.
trustVsCrypto.tableCryptoIncentives: The guarantee holds. No operator, no governance change can override what mathematics enforces.
trustVsCrypto.tableTrustVendor: Privacy depends on the continued good faith of a specific company or consortium
trustVsCrypto.tableCryptoVendor: Privacy is enforced by the protocol itself — no counterparty required
trustVsCrypto.tableTrustRegulatory: Operators can be compelled to provide access; privacy is a policy decision
trustVsCrypto.tableCryptoRegulatory: Selective disclosure enables compliance proofs without revealing underlying data
trustVsCrypto.closing: If you trust consortium operators to run the network honestly, respect your data boundaries, and not change the rules against your interests — you are back to trusting people. At that point, the question is: why not a database with legal contracts?

privateChains.heading: The Problem with Private Chains
privateChains.description: Permissioned blockchains were pitched as the enterprise answer to the privacy concerns of using public chains. After a decade of deployments, the results tell a different story. Institutions evaluating private blockchain infrastructure should consider the risks that have surfaced in production.
privateChains.vendorDependencyDesc: Private chains are built and maintained by a single company. If they pivot strategy, get acquired, or fail, your settlement layer is at risk. This is vendor lock-in at the most critical layer of your technology stack.
privateChains.noInteropDesc: Assets minted on a private chain exist in a gated ecosystem. No access to DeFi liquidity, no composability with other protocols, no secondary market, no utility.
privateChains.talentDesc: Proprietary languages and frameworks restrict your hiring pool. The EVM ecosystem has 8,000+ monthly active developers; proprietary alternatives have many orders of magnitude fewer. The result: slower iteration, higher cost, and difficulty retaining engineering talent.
privateChains.fragilityDesc: Private chain deployments have experienced multi-month outages from database corruption. External database dependencies (e.g. PostgreSQL sidecars used to index and locate contracts) create single points of failure. If that database is lost or corrupted, on-ledger access to contracts is effectively blocked.
privateChains.instabilityDesc: Runtime migrations, architecture pivots, and strategy shifts disrupt enterprise deployments. Institutions need infrastructure they can underwrite for a decade. Private chains have not demonstrated that stability.
privateChains.auditabilityDesc: Archived contracts not accessible on-ledger. Separate storage infrastructure required for complete audit trails, creating compliance risk for regulated institutions that need full transaction history on demand.
privateChains.offChainPrivacy: Privacy That Depends on Offchain Components
privateChains.offChainPrivacyDesc: On private chains, privacy often leans on offchain components for core operations, breaking the expectation that a distributed application can operate independently on-ledger. This is not true cryptographic privacy; it is architectural complexity disguised as a feature.
privateChains.missingAbstractionsDesc: Enterprises are forced to design custom patterns separating mutable data (with changing IDs) from stable business logic just to anchor references. Native abstractions — equivalent to EVM proxy patterns — simply do not exist, pushing complexity onto application developers.
privateChains.closing: Private chains recreate the same intermediary risk that blockchain technology was designed to eliminate. Slightly better plumbing is not a paradigm shift.

efCommitment.heading: The Ethereum Foundation's Privacy Commitment
efCommitment.description: Privacy is a tier-one priority for the Ethereum Foundation. Since 2015, the EF has had a dedicated 50-person Privacy team composed of leading cryptographic researchers and developers.
efCommitment.iptfDesc: Coordinates research across private payments, selective disclosure, zero-knowledge identity, and privacy-preserving applications on L2. The team ensures solutions meet regulatory and compliance requirements, turning institutional feedback into technical specifications and POCs on Ethereum.

metadata.title: Privacy & Compliance for Institutions | Ethereum
metadata.description: Settle onchain while protecting sensitive business data. Explore Ethereum's privacy features, from ZK proofs to FHE, for institutional regulatory, risk, and audit needs.

hero.heading: Library: Institutional Insights
hero.description1: Reports, articles, and analyses from across the institutional landscape, along with thought leadership and updates from the Ethereum Foundation's Enterprise Acceleration team.
hero.description2: Explore market trends, technical developments, and strategic opportunities for institutions building in the onchain economy.

metadata.title: Research & Insights | Ethereum Institutional Resources
metadata.description: Explore a curated library for businesses adopting Ethereum, with reports, analysis, market trends, and the latest institutional insights.

hero.heading: The Home of Decentralized Finance
hero.description1: Ethereum introduced the world to decentralized finance (DeFi): open financial systems built on programmable smart contracts.
hero.description2: 100% uptime, battle-tested and secure infrastructure, and the deepest liquidity layer of any blockchain—Ethereum is for DeFi.

primitives.heading: Ethereum is the Platform for DeFi Primitives
primitives.description: Ethereum's battle-tested, always-on infrastructure secures by far the largest pool of digital assets and liquidity, powered by the most developers and the broadest blockchain ecosystem. Ethereum doesn't lock users into a single blockchain operating environment, fostering a unified, global ecosystem where innovation and liquidity compound.
primitives.openStandardsDesc: Ethereum's shared EVM, token, and smart contract standards provide interoperability across the entire Ethereum DeFi ecosystem
primitives.deepLiquidityDesc: Ethereum has the deepest DeFi liquidity of any blockchain, hosting an efficient global marketplace for digital assets
primitives.composableDesc: Open-source building blocks and protocols act as 'money legos' that developers can combine to build sophisticated product which is not currently possible on traditional rails
primitives.permissionlessDesc: Anyone can build or access DeFi instruments, unlocking a global dynamic landscape of innovation without gatekeepers

innovation.heading: DeFi Powers Enterprise Innovation
innovation.description: Institutions build on Ethereum's open DeFi primitives to advance transparent, efficient, and accessible tools for a global financial system

mas.heading: Monetary Authority of Singapore & J.P. Morgan
mas.description: Live foreign exchange transaction executed via Aave on Polygon L2 mainnet

siemens.heading: Siemens
siemens.description: €60M digital bond issued onchain, sold directly to investors without engaging central securities depositories

visa.heading: Visa
visa.description: Visa Tokenized Asset Platform (VTAP) launched for financial institutions to issue fiat-backed ERC-20 tokens on Ethereum

socgen.heading: Société Générale
socgen.description: Euro and dollar stablecoins integrated with Uniswap and Morpho, for institutional clients to swap, lend, and borrow onchain

ecosystem.heading: Ethereum's DeFi Ecosystem
ecosystem.description: A small selection of DeFi applications that run on Ethereum and its Layer 2 networks

dex.heading: Decentralized Exchanges (DEXs)
dex.subtext: Swap digital assets through smart contract-based liquidity pools and automated market makers (AMMs)

platforms.oneInch: A DEX aggregator that sources liquidity from across DEXs and networks to find the best possible swapping routes and rates.
platforms.aerodrome: A decentralized exchange on the Base L2 that combines an AMM with a governance and protocol reward model designed to align incentives.
platforms.balancer: A DeFi protocol that acts as an automated portfolio manager and liquidity provider, allowing users to create or invest in customizable liquidity pools.
platforms.cowSwap: A DEX aggregator built around frequent batch auctions to provide MEV protection, find optimal liquidity, and minimize fees, matching trades peer-to-peer and sending unfilled orders to underlying AMMs.
platforms.curve: A DEX and AMM focused on efficient trading for stablecoins and volatile tokens, using a unique AMM formula to minimize slippage for similarly-priced assets.
platforms.fluid: A DeFi protocol that combines AMM trading with lending and borrowing on a unified liquidity layer, enabling capital-efficient swaps with deep, shared liquidity.
platforms.uniswap: A DEX protocol that enables permissionless peer-to-peer trading, through a smart contract-based AMM system that allows anyone to create custom pairs and supply liquidity.

lending-borrowing.heading: Lending & Borrowing
lending-borrowing.subtext: Borrow against onchain collateral and earn yield for supplying liquidity, via smart contracts with programmable terms

platforms.aave: A non-custodial protocol where users can create liquidity markets and participate as suppliers or borrowers, earning interest on supplied digital assets and borrowing against them.
platforms.compound: An algorithmic, autonomous interest rate protocol that allows users to earn interest by lending digital assets to borrowers, designed for developers to build DeFi applications on top of.
platforms.euler: A permissionless protocol for lending, borrowing, and swapping digital assets, featuring asset tiers and reactive interest rates to manage risk, with a modular lending platform for developers to create and connect custom lending vaults.
platforms.fluid: A DeFi protocol that offers lending, borrowing, and trading on top of a unified liquidity layer, allowing users to maximize yield and optimize capital efficiency through integrated debt and liquidity products.
platforms.maple: A decentralized credit market that provides undercollateralized loans to institutional borrowers, and allows lenders to earn yield by supplying liquidity to these curated loans.
platforms.moonwell: An open lending and borrowing protocol designed to simplify the onchain user experience, featuring single-sided liquidity pools and overcollateralized borrowing across markets.
platforms.morpho: A decentralized protocol for overcollateralized, vault-based lending and borrowing, aiming to improve the capital efficiency of lending pools through isolated markets, risk curators, and portfolio allocators.
platforms.sky: A DeFi protocol (created by the MakerDAO community) that offers a suite of tools for trading, converting, staking, and earning yield from digital assets, including the USDS stablecoin.
platforms.spark: A decentralized lending protocol offering competitive rates for borrowing and supplying major digital assets, with a focus on stablecoin markets and institutional-grade lending products.

rwa-yield.heading: Tokenized Assets & Onchain Yield
rwa-yield.subtext: Tokenized offchain assets, like T-Bills, commodities, or real estate, and onchain comparables to familiar yield instruments

platforms.centrifuge: An asset-agnostic lending protocol that aims to make credit more accessible to businesses, allowing them to tokenize real-world assets and use them as onchain collateral.
platforms.circle: A global financial technology firm that provides payments and financial infrastructure, tokenized money market funds, and more, best known for issuing the fully-regulated USD Coin (USDC) stablecoin.
platforms.ethena: A synthetic dollar protocol focused on providing a crypto-native solution for money, offering USDe, a stablecoin backed by crypto assets and corresponding short futures positions, and a dollar-denominated savings account.
platforms.ftBenji: A mobile app that allows investors to browse tokenized securities, digital assets, and invest in the Franklin OnChain U.S. Government Money Fund (BENJI), the first tokenized money fund natively issued on a blockchain.
platforms.frax: A fractional-algorithmic stablecoin ecosystem that aims to provide scalable, decentralized, and algorithmic money in place of fixed-supply digital assets.
platforms.ondo: A DeFi protocol focused on making institutional-grade financial products and services, like tokenized U.S. Treasuries, accessible onchain.
platforms.pendle: A DeFi protocol for trading interest rate derivatives via the tokenization of future yield, allowing users to lock in future yield from yield-bearing tokens, speculate on yield movements, and provide liquidity for yield-bearing tokens.
platforms.securitize: A platform for tokenizing real-world assets and issuing and managing digital asset securities onchain, allowing businesses to raise capital and investors to trade tokenized shares in funds and private companies.
platforms.skyRwa: A DeFi protocol (created by the MakerDAO community) that offers a suite of tools for trading, converting, staking, and earning yield from digital assets, including the USDS stablecoin.
platforms.tether: Issuer of the the USDT stablecoin, a stablecoin pegged to the U.S. dollar that's focused on using fiat currencies onchain.
platforms.veda: A vault infrastructure platform for institutions to launch and manage custom onchain products at scale.
platforms.wisdomTree: A global asset manager that offers a range of exchange-traded products (ETPs), including physically-backed crypto ETPs.

staking.heading: Staking & Restaking
staking.subtext: Stake ETH through a staking network to help secure Ethereum and earn rewards, or restake Liquid Staking Tokens (LSTs) to help secure other protocols and earn additional rewards

platforms.etherFi: A restaking platform to earn additional rewards on stablecoins, liquid staking tokens, and other digital assets, with deposits represented by a liquid restaking token that can be used across DeFi, and automated strategy vaults.
platforms.eigenCloud: A platform built on EigenLayer restaking that provides verifiable computing for AI and other complex applications, leveraging Ethereum's security through restaked ETH and restaked liquid staking tokens.
platforms.lido: A DAO-managed liquid staking network that allows users to stake their ETH and other PoS assets to earn staking rewards, with staked assets represented by stETH, a liquid staking token that can be used in DeFi and is issued to represent staking rewards.
platforms.liquidCollective: A liquid staking network built to provide a secure, decentralized way to stake ETH across operators and platforms, with staked assets represented by LsETH, a liquid staking token that can be used in DeFi while accruing staking rewards that's designed to meet institutional security and compliance requirements.
platforms.rocketPool: A liquid staking protocol focused on decentralization and censorship resistance, allowing users with as little as 0.01 ETH to participate in staking and receive a liquid staking token (rETH) that can be used in DeFi while it continues to accrue staking rewards.
platforms.stakewise: A liquid staking platform that offers both solo and pooled staking options, backed by a decentralized network of custom staking vaults, with a unified liquid staking token (osETH) that can be used in DeFi while it continues to accrue staking rewards.
platforms.symbiotic: A restaking protocol that enables users to secure additional blockchain networks by restaking their existing staked assets and liquid staking tokens, with a flexible and modular system for networks to manage independent rewards and penalties.

synthetic.heading: Predictions & Synthetics
synthetic.subtext: Trade on the outcome of future events via smart contract, or access tokens that track the value of any asset

platforms.polymarket: A decentralized prediction market where users can bet on the outcomes of real-world events by buying and selling shares in different possible outcomes.
platforms.synthetix: A liquidity protocol for creating synthetic digital assets that track the value of any asset, allowing users to gain exposure to a wide range of instruments without holding them directly.

metadata.title: Decentralized Finance for Institutions | Ethereum DeFi
metadata.description: Explore Ethereum's DeFi ecosystem, home to the deepest liquidity onchain. Learn how open standards power enterprise innovation in lending, borrowing & trading.

hero.description2: Explore curated metrics for mainnet activity, L2 scaling, DeFi markets, tokenized assets, and more.

metadata.title: Live Ethereum Network Data | Institutional Onchain Data Hub
metadata.description: Get live intelligence for the onchain economy. Explore curated metrics for mainnet activity, L2 scaling, DeFi markets, tokenized assets, and more.

hero.heading: Ethereum L2s
hero.description1: Layer 2s (L2s) are scalable and customizable layers built on top of Ethereum. Rollups, a primary category of L2, bundle transactions and post data back to Ethereum mainnet, allowing them to inherit Ethereum's security and resilience.
hero.description2: Suitable for tokenization, payments, and compliant appchains, L2s can offer custom environments, optimized rails and execution, while relying on Ethereum for finality and security.

l1.heading: Why L2s Exist
l1.description: Ethereum L1 is highly secure and single-operator independent, but its challenges include:
l1.closing: While Ethereum's network upgrades are rapidly scaling L1 capacity today, L2s offer an immediate solution.

l2.heading: How L2s Work
l2.description: L2s offer fast, low-cost transaction execution, while Ethereum acts as the ultimate source of truth for settlement:
l2.item1: Users send transaction processing to the L2 network, instead of directly on Ethereum
l2.item2: The L2 batches and/or compresses transactions, executes them offchain, and in turn sends the data back to the L1 as a source of truth and global consensus

noBootstrapping.heading: No Bootstrapping
noBootstrapping.item1: Skip the costly process of building and paying a new validator set to economically secure a chain
noBootstrapping.item2: New chains must build decentralization from scratch, a slow, capital-intensive process that can lead to fragile and centralized chains
noBootstrapping.item3: L2s inherit Ethereum's unmatched decentralization, saving vital resources, and accessing deep economic security

sharedSecurity.heading: Shared Security
sharedSecurity.item1: L2s tap into <link>{securityValue}+ in economic security</link>, paying only usage-based fees.
sharedSecurity.item2: Rollups all settle on Ethereum, creating a shared, decentralized security and data availability layer

noInflation.heading: No Inflation Burden
noInflation.item1: Alternative L1s pay for security and usage via high native token inflation rates
noInflation.item2: L2s pay usage-based fees in ETH when sending transaction data back to the L1, removing the need for a native-L2 token to facilitate economic security
noInflation.item3: Because L2s don't need to use a native token to pay for security, they can choose to use a native token for other strategic functions, like governance, incentives, or transaction fees on the L2
noInflation.item4: High levels of profitability (e.g. 90%+ margins on Base)

highPerformance.heading: High-Performance Validation
highPerformance.item1: By separating execution from consensus and inheriting Ethereum's finality, rollups enable high performance without sacrificing user safety

fasterCheaper.heading: Faster & Cheaper
fasterCheaper.item1: Rollups can parallelize execution and proving—they don't need to wait for thousands of nodes to sync
fasterCheaper.item2: One proof replaces millions of redundant re-computations across nodes, greatly reducing validation costs

saferValidation.heading: Safer Validation
saferValidation.item1: Rollups inherit Ethereum's censorship resistance and finality
saferValidation.item2: Assets are held in Ethereum contracts, and updates are enforced via fraud or validity proofs
saferValidation.item3: Even if the L2 sequencer misbehaves, users can force transaction inclusion and securely exit via Ethereum L1, enabling a fail safe and extra layer of security

sharedStandards.heading: Shared Standards
sharedStandards.item1: L2s leverage Ethereum's established EVM, token standards, and core assets like ETH
sharedStandards.item2: Users and developers benefit from a consistent experience, using the same wallets, tools, and code across Ethereum's L1 and L2 ecosystem
sharedStandards.item3: Tokens maintain a canonical L1 source-of-truth, making them fungible across different rollups

configurableEnvironments.heading: Customizable Environments
configurableEnvironments.item1: Institutions can design rollups with custom built-in compliance logic, embedding specific identity verification protocols, permissioned access, or privacy controls directly into the L2 architecture
configurableEnvironments.item2: Launch dedicated L2 ecosystems to maintain a unique brand identity and control the end-to-end user experience onchain, as demonstrated by L2 networks like Base and Robinhood Chain
configurableEnvironments.item3: L2 parameters can be optimized for throughput, such as guaranteeing dedicated block space, high transaction speeds, and predictable fees tailored to high-volume enterprise applications

trust.heading: Ethereum as the Trust Layer
trust.description: Rollups are the most secure form of L2, where transactions are executed offchain but the data is published to Ethereum L1
trust.transparencyDesc: Every user can see the rollup state on the L1
trust.dataAvailabilityDesc: Even if the rollup sequencer disappears or censors users, the entire history of transactions is accessible and immutable on Ethereum
trust.exitWindowDesc: Anyone can directly withdraw their assets from the rollup to the L1 by interacting with the L1 smart contract
trust.recoverabilityDesc: Referred to as the "escape hatch", data availability on the L1 makes user funds recoverable even if the rollup fails, making rollups trust-minimized rather than reliant on the rollup operator

issuance.heading: Issuance on L1 (Bridging to L2)
issuance.description: Assets should be issued on the L1 and bridged to the L2:
issuance.anchors: Anchors assets to Ethereum's most secure, neutral and resilient layer
issuance.maximizes: Maximises interoperability and composability across Ethereum ecosystem

frameworksSection.heading: Simplified Deployment and Flexibility
frameworksSection.description: Launch your own L2 or deploy on an existing L2.

frameworks.arbitrum:
Arbitrum One delivers strong security, fast confirmations, and low fees with straightforward deployment and a bustling DeFi ecosystem. Optionally allowing for teams to launch here first, then migrate to their own custom Arbitrum chain later.
Arbitrum offers a purpose-built stack for customizable chains, with full support for Solidity and Rust and access to liquidity from the parent chain, like Ethereum or Arbitrum One.
frameworks.optimism: Optimism inherits Ethereum's data availability and settlement guarantees as an optimistic rollup, with EVM equivalence ensuring existing contracts and tooling work out of the box. Teams can deploy directly on OP Mainnet for immediate access to established liquidity and users, or launch a dedicated OP Stack chain for greater control over throughput, fees, and governance.
frameworks.polygon: Polygon protocols and scaling technologies are used to build and deploy apps, or launch ZK rollups and validiums as L2s.
frameworks.zksync:
ZKsync provides cryptographic settlement guarantees through proofs, with every transaction batch mathematically verified before posting to Ethereum. Teams can deploy on ZKsync chains for access to L1 liquidity and ecosystem tooling, or launch a dedicated chain for greater sovereignty over performance, governance, and fees.
For institutions requiring privacy and compliance, ZKsync offers Prividiums, an Ethereum secured blockchain platform purpose built for organisations that demand privacy, compliance, and full control of their data.

networks.linea: General purpose, EVM equivalent L2, built by Consensys.
networks.starknet: Validity rollup (ZK-Rollup) L2 using STARK proofs. High-throughput, low-cost.
networks.base: Secure, low-cost L2 built on OP Stack by Coinbase.
networks.ink: L2 built by Kraken, bridging CeFi with onchain apps.
networks.unichain: DeFi-centered L2 for high-throughput and cross-chain liquidity.
networks.scroll: zkEVM-based L2 for full Ethereum compatibility.

world.description: World Chain L2 to scale Proof of Human

existing.heading: Use an Existing L2
existing.immediateAccessDesc: Tap into mature ecosystems with large developer communities and liquidity networks.
existing.lowerCostsDesc: Inherit Ethereum's decentralisation and security—no need to bootstrap infrastructure.
existing.regulatoryAlignmentDesc: Many L2s are already building compliance modules suited for financial institutions.
existing.productionSecurityDesc: Build on live and battle-tested L2 chains, with billions of dollars in value secured.

custom.heading: Launch a Custom L2
custom.tailoredEnvironmentsDesc: Deploy a private or public rollup using existing stacks, tailored to your specific enterprise needs.
custom.customFeaturesDesc: Implement permissioned access, privacy layers, compliance hooks, or internal system integration.
custom.sharedSecurityDesc: Even private rollups settle to Ethereum, benefiting from its validator set without the cost of building one.
custom.fasterTimeToMarketDesc: Build using audited, battle-tested rollup frameworks to deploy in weeks, not years.

metadata.title: Ethereum's Layer 2 Ecosystem | Scalable Enterprise Solutions
metadata.description: Layer 2s offer faster, cheaper execution, ideal for payments, appchains, and tokenization. Learn how L2s help institutions scale without sacrificing security.

hero.heading: Why Ethereum
hero.description1: Global financial markets continue to rely on outdated, costly, and fragmented settlement systems that introduce operational risk, capital inefficiency, and reconciliation overhead.
hero.description2: Cross-border transactions remain slow and opaque, while legacy infrastructure limits programmability, transparency, and real-time auditability.

pressures.heading: Pressure Drivers of
pressures.efficiencyDesc: Firms are under pressure to compress settlement windows and reduce the capital trapped in counterparty risk buffers.
pressures.transparencyDesc: Regulators and risk teams increasingly expect real-time, verifiable records over batch-processed reconciliation.
pressures.digitalAssetsDesc: Client demand for tokenized funds, onchain treasuries, and digital securities requires infrastructure that supports these products within existing regulatory frameworks.
pressures.openInfraDesc: Institutions are seeking neutral, standards-based settlement layers that avoid proprietary lock-in and enable interoperability across providers.

limitations.heading: Current
limitations.fragmentedDesc: Settlement remains siloed across counterparties, internal operations, and jurisdictions, requiring manual coordination at every boundary.
limitations.delayDesc: T+2 and T+3 cycles lock up capital, amplify counterparty exposure, and introduce systemic risk during volatile markets.
limitations.reconciliationDesc: Maintaining consistent records across participants demands costly reconciliation and audit processes that scale poorly.
limitations.programmabilityDesc: Legacy rails offer minimal support for conditional logic, automated compliance, or composable financial products.
limitations.opacityDesc: Participants lack real-time visibility into counterparty positions and settlement status, forcing reliance on trust and periodic reporting.

features.heading: Why Institutions Choose Ethereum
features.description: Ethereum is an open, globally distributed settlement network designed to support programmable financial activity. Unlike proprietary or permissioned systems, Ethereum operates as a neutral base layer with no single operator, while enabling institutions to build compliance-aware applications on top.

resilience.label: Operational Resilience
resilience.description: {uptime} of uninterrupted uptime and liveness, including through 15+ network upgrades. Diverse infrastructure and an open client ecosystem reduce infrastructure concentration and  single-operator risks.

settlement.label: Global Settlement
settlement.description: Ethereum settles value continuously across jurisdictions, with transparent finality and publicly verifiable execution.

collateral.label: Collateral Mobility
collateral.description: Reliable collateral movement whenever you need it through Ethereum's always-on collateral markets, proven for over a decade.

neutrality.label: Neutrality
neutrality.description: No single point of failure, no central coordinator, no pause button, no counterparty risk. Resilient to geopolitical, regulatory, and infrastructure-level risks.

liquidity.label: Liquidity and Utility
liquidity.description: The leading platform for asset tokenization with 67% of all tokenized assets deployed. Benefitting from 10 years of network effects, Ethereum's DeFi ecosystem is 8x larger than the next largest.

programmability.label: Programmability
programmability.description: Smart contracts enable automated issuance, transfer controls, lifecycle management, and conditional settlement. Apps and Layer 2 networks can be customized to the exact requirements of financial institutions.

composability.label: Composable Ecosystem
composability.description: Ethereum's ecosystem of institutional-grade wallets, custodians, auditors, data providers, and scaling solutions support billions in institutional capital.

risk.heading: Ethereum For Risk Management
risk.description: Ethereum is a powerful foundation, but not a turnkey solution. Successful adoption typically occurs alongside existing infrastructure, not as a wholesale replacement, and requires careful evaluation.
risk.item2: Transaction fees can fluctuate under network demand
risk.item3: Public transaction visibility requires privacy-preserving design choices
risk.item4: Smart contract risk requires rigorous audit and control processes

mitigation.heading: Mitigation Approaches
mitigation.item2: Use of Layer 2 networks and transaction batching
mitigation.item4: Permissioned vs public chains via Layer 2 customization
mitigation.item6: Institutional custody and key management solutions
mitigation.item7: Legal structuring aligned with local regulatory guidance

risk.closing: Modernizing settlement infrastructure is a deliberate process. Institutions can adopt incrementally, starting with specific use cases and expanding as confidence and regulatory clarity grow.

metadata.title: Why Ethereum for Institutions | The Case for Ethereum
metadata.description: Understand why leading institutions choose Ethereum for settlement, tokenization, and financial infrastructure. Operational resilience, neutrality, and deep liquidity.

hero.heading: Global Solution Providers
hero.description: Connect with a curated network of solution providers that specialize in building in the Ethereum ecosystem. From tokenization platforms to infrastructure and compliance, find established, regional teams to support your onchain strategy.

filters.searchPlaceholder: Search providers by name, category, or description...

metadata.title: Global Solution Providers | Ethereum Institutional Ecosystem
metadata.description: Find infrastructure, tooling, advisory, and compliance service providers in the Ethereum ecosystem for institutional needs.
